preamble: The shift from the high-back rounded vowel [u] (or its semivowel counterpart [w]) to the labiodental fricative [v] is a common form of consonantalization. This process occurred independently in both the Romance and Germanic language families as glides strengthened into obstruents.

examples.note: Latin semivocalic V strengthened to a fricative.
German shifted the Proto-Germanic glide to a labiodental fricative.
